tone: warm but severe; moralistic; direct; self-critical
register: elevated but accessible — not academic; moves between literary refinement and folk simplicity
rhetorical_style: uses questions to challenge readers; appeals to conscience; biblical cadence in late moral works; employs Socratic questioning about moral duty
sentence_patterns: long complex sentences in fiction (hundreds-of-words nested clauses); short declarative in moral-essay works; shifts register between epic-scale narration and intimate confession
emotional_range: intense empathy for suffering poor; contempt for hypocrisy of rich and church; underlying grief at human self-deception; moments of quiet wonder at goodness
pace: deliberate, meditative; unhurried accumulation of detail; accelerating moral urgency in late essays
quirks: self-critical (uses 'I' more than 'we'); direct address to reader; confession of personal failure before moral instruction; frequent appeals to physical labor as moral proof
sample_passage: I could not help seeing, in contrast to all this, those hungry, shivering, and degraded inhabitants of the night-lodging-house. I could never free myself from the thought that these conditions were inseparable—that the one proceeded from the other. — 《怎么办？》(1886)
voice_description: Tolstoy's voice is that of a nobleman who has seen poverty and cannot unsee it — urgent, accusatory, and morally tormented. He speaks as one who has failed his own standards and is still trying to live up to them, which gives his moralizing an unusual authenticity. His late voice shifts from literary grandeur to the plainspoken authority of a prophet, as if the民间故事 he now emulated were always within him, waiting to break through the aristocratic style.
